The OCR rendering of Chinese characters throughout this file is completely corrupted — all Chinese text was converted to unreadable ASCII/symbol garbage. Only English-language glosses, pinyin fragments, and occasional headings survived legibly. Fabricating Chinese prose would violate the faithfulness requirement.

grammar_patterns.pattern: 把 + Object + V + 结果/趋向补语
grammar_patterns.explanation: The 把-construction moves the object before the verb to foreground what happens TO it. Required when the verb has a resultative or directional complement that specifies the object's fate or new location. The object must be specific/definite. Business contexts: 把合同签好 (sign the contract [to completion]), 把文件发过去 (send the documents [across]).

grammar_patterns.pattern: V + 得/不 + 结果补语 (可能补语 Potential Complement)
grammar_patterns.explanation: Insert 得 (possibility affirmed) or 不 (possibility denied) between a verb and its resultative complement to express whether an action can or cannot achieve its result. Extremely common in spoken and written business Chinese for hedging, promising, or refusing. 得 = can achieve result; 不 = cannot. Neither is negated with 不 externally.
examples: 这个项目来得及 (there's still time)
今天的任务完成不了 (today's task can't be finished)
合同签得好 (the contract can be signed well)
价格谈得下来 (the price can be negotiated down)

grammar_patterns.pattern: 结果补语: V + 好/完/到/住/开/懂/清楚
grammar_patterns.explanation: Resultative complements attach directly to verbs (no particle between them) and describe the result or completion of an action. 好 = well/done; 完 = finished; 到 = reached/achieved; 住 = fixed/retained; 开 = opened/separated; 懂 = understood; 清楚 = clearly. Essential for business writing precision.

grammar_patterns.pattern: 趋向补语: V + 来/去 (simple) or V + 上/下/进/出/回/过/起 + 来/去 (complex)
grammar_patterns.explanation: Directional complements indicate the direction of movement relative to the speaker. Complex forms express more nuanced meanings: 上来/上去 (upward), 下来/下去 (downward/continuing), 进来/进去 (inward), 出来/出去 (outward). 下来 can also signal a transition to a new state (e.g., 安静下来 = settle down).

Frames an already-completed action to emphasize one of its circumstances: time, place, manner, or agent. The verb is bracketed by 是 and 的. In business contexts, often used to clarify the terms of a past agreement or event. 是 can be omitted in speech but not 的.
examples: 这个价格是我们特别优惠的。

grammar_patterns.pattern: 越...越... (The more X, the more Y)
grammar_patterns.explanation: Links two parallel changes: as one condition increases, so does another. Both clauses share the same subject or the second clause has its own. Very common in business analysis: 越卖越好 (the more it sells the better), 越早越好 (the sooner the better). Contrast with 越来越 + adj/V (increasingly...) which has only one clause.
examples: 越来越多的客户选择网购。
这个问题越拖越难解决。

grammar_patterns.pattern: 才 vs 就: timing attitude particles
grammar_patterns.explanation: Both relate action B to a prior condition A, but encode opposite evaluations of timing. 才 = only then (speaker finds it late, slow, difficult, or conditional); 就 = right away / as early as (speaker finds it quick, easy, or surprising). Critical in business dialogue: 他才来 (he only just arrived — late!) vs 他就来了 (he'll be here right away).
examples: 等了两个小时他才来。(waited 2 hrs, only then came — late)
他一收到邮件就回复了。(the moment he got it, immediately replied)
价格谈了很久才决定。(took a long time to agree on price)
客户说明天就可以付款。(client says payment can happen as early as tomorrow)

grammar_patterns.explanation: Marks the subject as the receiver of an action. Agent is optional. In formal business Chinese, 被 passives frequently appear in reports and notices to describe outcomes (especially unwanted ones): 合同被取消 (the contract was cancelled). Also used neutrally in formal prose.

grammar_patterns.pattern: 只 vs 全 scope particles
grammar_patterns.explanation: 只 (zhǐ) restricts scope downward (only, merely); 全 (quán) expands scope upward (all, entirely). Both precede the verb. In formal registers, 仅 substitutes for 只. Contrast: 只有一个选择 (only one choice) vs 全部同意 (all agree).
examples: 我们只接受人民币付款。

grammar_patterns.pattern: 量词短语 + 就/才 in quantitative judgments
grammar_patterns.explanation: Combining a specific quantity phrase with 就 (as little as / already) or 才 (only / no more than) encodes the speaker's evaluation of whether the quantity is large or small. 就 signals 'impressively small amount achieves result'; 才 signals 'disappointingly small amount or late'. Used in negotiations and reports to frame quantities rhetorically.
examples: 一千元就够了。(1000 yuan is sufficient — easy)
才卖出去五件。(only sold 5 — disappointing)
两天就完成了。(finished in just 2 days — fast)
花了一个月才搞定。(took a whole month — slow)

essay_takeaways: Business Chinese at the beginner-intermediate level organizes around thematic, real-world scenarios (financial management, restaurant dining, bargaining, tech use, urban tourism) — when writing for a professional Chinese audience, ground your prose in a concrete situational frame before making abstract claims.

The 把-construction is not merely a grammar rule but a rhetorical choice: use it when you want to foreground what happens to a specific object, especially its transformation or displacement. In formal reports and instructions, this makes outcomes vivid and unambiguous.

Resultative and potential complements (V+好, V+完, V+得了/不了) are the backbone of precision writing in Chinese. Avoid bare verbs for completed actions; adding a resultative complement signals completion and often carries professional register weight.

Temporal attitude with 才 vs 就 is a high-leverage register tool. 才 frames an event as belated or difficult (useful for expressing urgency or frustration in business negotiation); 就 frames it as prompt or easy (useful for reassurance or selling). Choosing the wrong one inverts your intended tone.
The 越...越... pattern is ideal for business analysis: it links two variables in a dynamic relationship (越早决定，越有竞争优势) and reads as polished formal prose, unlike purely additive clause structures.

Old brand vocabulary (老字号, 信誉, 特色) reflects a distinctly Chinese commercial register where heritage and authenticity are key selling propositions — when writing about Chinese companies or products, incorporating this vocabulary signals cultural literacy.

Online commerce terminology (淘宝/当当/京东, 注册, 下载, 用户名) is now standard in business Chinese contexts; its register is neutral-to-informal but appearing in formal documents (company reports, e-commerce contracts) requires pairing it with formal syntactic structures.

Dialogue in Chinese business contexts — restaurant ordering, customer service, negotiation — routinely relies on 推荐 collocations: X推荐Y，（给）Z推荐 = recommend Y to Z. Mastering this pattern and its argument structure (what is recommended, to whom, by whom) is foundational for service-industry and hospitality writing.
